document.title: January 9, 2023 Meeting of the Dermatologic and Ophthalmic Drugs Advisory Committee- FDA Briefing Document

key_concerns: Primary non-inferiority endpoint not met in either pivotal study
High rate of important protocol deviations in Butterfleye (40% in aflibercept arm)
Potential systemic safety risks in preterm infants (higher systemic exposure than adults, nonclinical nasal turbinate erosions)
Safety database for pediatric ROP limited; long-term effects unknown
key_positives: Orphan drug designation shows recognition of unmet need
Avoids retinal laser scarring, potentially preserving peripheral vision
Single injection may reduce treatment burden compared to multiple laser sessions

key_concerns: Primary non-inferiority endpoint not met in either pivotal study
High rate of important protocol deviations in Butterfleye (40% in aflibercept arm)
Potential systemic safety risks in preterm infants (higher systemic exposure than adults, nonclinical nasal turbinate erosions)
Safety database for pediatric ROP limited; long-term effects unknown
key_positives: Orphan drug designation shows recognition of unmet need
Avoids retinal laser scarring, potentially preserving peripheral vision
Single injection may reduce treatment burden compared to multiple laser sessions
evidence_notes: "the primary endpoint (non-inferiority to the control) was not met in either study"
"Failure of the Primary Endpoint precludes testing of the secondary or other endpoints"
"BUTTERFLEYE: Any Important Protocol Deviation ... 40%" in aflibercept arm
"concentrations of free aflibercept in plasma ... 0.583 mcg/mL at Day 1" — exceeding adult levels
"Erosions and ulcerations of the respiratory epithelium in nasal turbinates in monkeys" — at exposures only 2-fold higher than infant Cmax

questions_framing.primary_question: Should the benefit-risk profile of aflibercept for ROP be considered favorable despite failure to demonstrate non-inferiority?
questions_framing.focus: Insufficient efficacy evidence and potential safety concerns in preterm infants

signal.label: STRONG_NEGATIVE
rationale: Concerns: Primary non-inferiority endpoint not met in either pivotal study; High rate of important protocol deviations in Butterfleye (40% in aflibercept arm); Potential systemic safety risks in preterm infants (higher systemic exposure than adults, nonclinical nasal turbinate erosions); Safety database for pediatric ROP limited; long-term effects unknown

Positives: Orphan drug designation shows recognition of unmet need; Avoids retinal laser scarring, potentially preserving peripheral vision; Single injection may reduce treatment burden compared to multiple laser sessions

document.title: FDA Briefing Information for the December 13, 2019 Meeting of the Dermatologic and Ophthalmic Drugs Advisory Committee

key_concerns: Small safety database (<90 subjects), limiting detection of rare adverse events
Hyperglycemia risk, especially in diabetics, requiring glucose monitoring
Hearing impairment (hypoacusis, tinnitus) with unknown mechanism
Higher infection rate vs placebo without clear cause
Undefined 'Active' Thyroid Eye Disease criteria for indication
Unknown long-term efficacy and safety of repeat treatment courses
key_positives: Significant proptosis reduction: 82% vs 16% placebo (2 mm threshold)
No approved drug therapies for Thyroid Eye Disease
Clinically meaningful improvement in diplopia and corneal coverage
Favorable effect on non-study eye (68% reduction)
Inclusion of patient-reported outcomes and clinician-reported outcomes

key_concerns: Small safety database (<90 subjects), limiting detection of rare adverse events
Hyperglycemia risk, especially in diabetics, requiring glucose monitoring
Hearing impairment (hypoacusis, tinnitus) with unknown mechanism
Higher infection rate vs placebo without clear cause
Undefined 'Active' Thyroid Eye Disease criteria for indication
Unknown long-term efficacy and safety of repeat treatment courses
key_positives: Significant proptosis reduction: 82% vs 16% placebo (2 mm threshold)
No approved drug therapies for Thyroid Eye Disease
Clinically meaningful improvement in diplopia and corneal coverage
Favorable effect on non-study eye (68% reduction)
Inclusion of patient-reported outcomes and clinician-reported outcomes
evidence_notes: Efficacy claim: 'Eighty-two percent (82%) of patients... had at least a two-millimeter reduction in ptosis [likely proptosis] compared to only 16 percent' (Section: Efficacy)

Clinical significance: 'A two-millimeter reduction is considered clinically significant because it is expected to reduce the incidence of diplopia and improve the lid coverage over the cornea' (Section: Efficacy)
Limited database: 'Less than 90 subjects treated with infusions of teprotumumab have been enrolled in controlled clinical trials' (Section: Efficacy C)
Safety signal: 'Based on the rule of 3’s, with a safety database of 90 subjects, adverse events may be expected to occur at rates of 3% without the event being observed' (Section: Efficacy C)
Hyperglycemia: 'Three teprotumumab subjects with normal fasting glucose values at Baseline demonstrated elevated fasting glucose values for at least 1 visit' (Section: Efficacy E)
Infection rate: 'The reported infection rate associated with teprotumumab was 33%, and higher than that of the placebo control in both studies' (Section: Safety I)
Hearing impairment: 'At least five patients reported hypoacusis/loss of hearing... The mechanism of action for hypoacusis remains unknown' (Section: Safety G)

questions_framing.description: FDA briefing poses neutral benefit-risk question ('Do the potential benefits... outweigh the potential risks?') and requests labeling recommendations, indicating standard advisory committee framing focused on approval conditions rather than outright rejection.

signal.label: STRONG_POSITIVE
rationale: Concerns: Small safety database (<90 subjects), limiting detection of rare adverse events; Hyperglycemia risk, especially in diabetics, requiring glucose monitoring; Hearing impairment (hypoacusis, tinnitus) with unknown mechanism; Higher infection rate vs placebo without clear cause; Undefined 'Active' Thyroid Eye Disease criteria for indication

Positives: Significant proptosis reduction: 82% vs 16% placebo (2 mm threshold); No approved drug therapies for Thyroid Eye Disease; Clinically meaningful improvement in diplopia and corneal coverage; Favorable effect on non-study eye (68% reduction); Inclusion of patient-reported outcomes and clinician-reported outcomes

document.title: May 9-10, 2023 Joint Meeting of the Nonprescription Drugs Advisory Committee and the Obstetrics, Reproductive and Urologic Drugs Advisory Committee- FDA Briefing Document

key_concerns: Improbable dosing data undermines adherence reliability
Inadequate deselection by breast cancer patients
Low comprehension of abnormal bleeding warning
Concomitant hormonal contraceptive use despite warnings
Adolescent comprehension gaps
Limited literacy underrepresentation
Unknown efficacy in overweight/obese population
key_positives: Potential to increase access to more effective contraception
Nonprescription availability may reduce unintended pregnancies

key_concerns: Improbable dosing data undermines adherence reliability
Inadequate deselection by breast cancer patients
Low comprehension of abnormal bleeding warning
Concomitant hormonal contraceptive use despite warnings
Adolescent comprehension gaps
Limited literacy underrepresentation
Unknown efficacy in overweight/obese population
key_positives: Potential to increase access to more effective contraception
Nonprescription availability may reduce unintended pregnancies
evidence_notes: 'Approximately 1/3 of participants had improbable dosing results' - calls into question reliability of actual use data.
'Comprehension of this contraindication did not approach the prespecified target threshold' - for breast cancer deselection.
'More than half of participants with unexplained vaginal bleeding incorrectly indicated norgestrel would be appropriate' - deselection failure.
'11 participants reported using a hormone-containing birth control product while using norgestrel' - safety concern.
'Younger adolescents (11-14) demonstrated low comprehension of important labeling messages' - adolescent risk.
'Limited literacy participation was only 5% in targeted breast cancer self-selection study' - representation concern.
'The degree to which efficacy is diminished in overweight or obese individuals remains unknown' - efficacy gap.

questions_framing.focus: FDA questions probe adequacy of consumer behavior evidence, emphasizing risks of self-selection errors and non-adherence, suggesting current data insufficient to support switch.

signal.label: STRONG_NEGATIVE
rationale: Concerns: Improbable dosing data undermines adherence reliability; Inadequate deselection by breast cancer patients; Low comprehension of abnormal bleeding warning; Concomitant hormonal contraceptive use despite warnings; Adolescent comprehension gaps
Positives: Potential to increase access to more effective contraception; Nonprescription availability may reduce unintended pregnancies

document.title: June 8, 2023 Meeting of the Antimicrobial Drugs Advisory Committee- FDA Briefing Document

key_concerns: Imbalance in deaths between treatment groups (details not provided in excerpt)
Efficacy in second RSV season limited due to low RSV circulation during COVID-19 pandemic
Hospitalization reduction in Trial 04 not statistically significant (p=0.09)
key_positives: High efficacy in reducing medically attended RSV LRTI in both preterm and term infants
Single-dose regimen offers convenience over monthly palivizumab
Favorable safety profile with mostly mild adverse events

key_concerns: Imbalance in deaths between treatment groups (details not provided in excerpt)
Efficacy in second RSV season limited due to low RSV circulation during COVID-19 pandemic
Hospitalization reduction in Trial 04 not statistically significant (p=0.09)
key_positives: High efficacy in reducing medically attended RSV LRTI in both preterm and term infants
Single-dose regimen offers convenience over monthly palivizumab
Favorable safety profile with mostly mild adverse events
evidence_notes: Nirsevimab showed 70.1% relative risk reduction of MA RSV LRTI in preterm infants ('relative risk reduction of 70.1%...p <0.0001')
Nirsevimab showed 74.9% relative risk reduction of MA RSV LRTI in term infants ('relative risk reduction of 74.9%...p <0.0001')

Hospitalization reduction was 78.4% in Trial 03 ('relative risk reduction of 78.4%...p=.0002') but 60.2% in Trial 04 with wide confidence interval crossing zero ('relative risk reduction of 60.2% (95% CI: -14.6% to 86.2%; p=0.09)')
Safety data pooled from 3 trials showed most common adverse reactions were rash and injection site reactions ('most common adverse reactions...rash (0.7%) and injection site reactions (0.4%)')

Second season efficacy data from Trial 05 showed no cases, but RSV circulation was low during pandemic ('There were no cases of MA RSV LRTI in the second RSV season, which was conducted in 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic')

The document notes an imbalance in deaths as a safety consideration without further detail ('Safety Considerations: • Hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis and rash • Imbalance in deaths')

questions_framing.remarks: Questions focus on benefit-risk, subpopulation appropriateness, and post-marketing considerations; they do not appear leading or negative.

signal.label: STRONG_POSITIVE
rationale: Concerns: Imbalance in deaths between treatment groups (details not provided in excerpt); Efficacy in second RSV season limited due to low RSV circulation during COVID-19 pandemic; Hospitalization reduction in Trial 04 not statistically significant (p=0.09)

Positives: High efficacy in reducing medically attended RSV LRTI in both preterm and term infants; Single-dose regimen offers convenience over monthly palivizumab; Favorable safety profile with mostly mild adverse events
